name: Possessive Adjectives
summary: Say my, your, his, and her with words that agree with the thing owned, not the owner.
explanation:
Possessive adjectives show ownership and agree with the noun that follows them, not with the owner. For 'my' you use mon (m), ma (f), or mes (plural): mon frère, ma sœur, mes parents.

The full set is: mon/ma/mes (my), ton/ta/tes (your, informal), son/sa/ses (his/her/its), notre/nos (our), votre/vos (your, formal/plural), leur/leurs (their).

A tricky point: son and sa mean both 'his' and 'her', because they agree with the object, not the owner. So sa voiture is 'his car' or 'her car' depending on context. Before a feminine noun starting with a vowel, use mon/ton/son for sound: mon amie.
pattern: mon/ma/mes, ton/ta/tes, son/sa/ses, notre/nos, votre/vos, leur/leurs

examples.en: He is looking for his key. (sa agrees with clé)
My friend is arriving. (mon before a feminine vowel)

commonMistakes: Matching the possessive to the owner instead of the owned noun: it is ma sœur because sœur is feminine.
Using sa for 'her' and son for 'his' as a fixed rule. Both depend on the noun's gender.
Using ma before a feminine noun starting with a vowel: it is mon amie, not ma amie.
Forgetting the plural forms mes, tes, ses, nos, vos, leurs.
Confusing leur (their, possessive) with leur (to them, pronoun).
tip: Ask what gender the owned noun is, not who owns it. Son livre is 'his or her book' because livre is masculine.
